title: Concurrent Exhibitions at List Gallery
description:
Join us for two concurrent exhibitions at the List Gallery: Paper Journeys: Pop-up Books and Constructions by Colette Fu and Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose: Selected East Asian Works from the Swarthmore College Art Collection. Together, these exhibitions explore current and historical East Asian works and themes, such as culture and identity, travel and its transformations, and the importance of diverse narratives. Paper Journeys features mixed-media works by Colette Fu, a Philadelphia-based artist who is known for her paper constructions that explore her Nuosu Yi ancestry and the cultural traditions of diverse ethnic minority groups in China. Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose features works from Swarthmore's College Art Collection and highlights concerns explored by East Asian artists from the 14th century to the mid-twentieth century. These interrelated exhibitions were co-curated and organized by Andrea Packard, Director of the Swarthmore College Art Collection and Curator of the List Gallery; Sara Hesdon Buehler, Art Collection Manager; and Susan Eberhard, Visiting Assistant Professor, Art History.

The exhibitions will be on view in the List Gallery from March 3 through April 19, 2026.
Colette Fu will give a talk about her work on Thursday, March 19 at 4:30 PM in the Lang Performing Arts Center Cinema; an opening reception will follow in the List Gallery from 5:30 to 7:00 PM.

title: Exhibition: McCabe Library–from Dirt to Dust
description:
Dedicated in 1967, the Thomas B. and Jeanette L. McCabe Library has a history that began millions of years earlier, with the schist that forms its walls, and that will end untold years in the future, as it returns to the earth. In between, it is a building constantly in flux. Created as an act of permanence, to preserve the books and archives within its walls, McCabe has had to constantly adapt to the changing ways that people use libraries. Built by skilled hands that honed it from wood, concrete, and stone, many more hands make sure every day that it retains its function.

Curated by students in ARTH 067: Building Architecture from Dirt to Dust, this exhibition examines these many hands, including quarrymen and construction workers, custodians and archivists, architects and journalists, donors and woodworkers. Using the very archival documents housed in McCabe and some beyond, students follow ten themes (dirt, material, design, construction, capital, furnishing and decor, media, renovation, maintenance and care, and dust) that illuminate the myriad people and processes that have shaped and reshaped this familiar structure. In telling the many histories of McCabe Library from dirt to dust, the objects in this exhibition tell a larger story about all buildings—as the products of diverse forms of labor, shaped collaboratively, and with long lives that extend far into the past and future.

On display in the McCabe Cratsley Lounge (2nd Floor) through March 1.

title: Arabic Language Table
description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. It fosters spontaneous speech, reinforces vocabulary in context, and encourages cultural exploration through discussions on music, films, and current events. title: Lee Frank Lecture: Making San Fran Chinatown with Marci Kwon
description:
An endowment by the family and friends of Lee Frank, Class of 1921, sponsors a special event each year : a visiting lecturer or artist, a scholar or artist in residence, or a special exhibit.

MARCI KWON
Associate Professor of Art & Art History, Stanford University
Making San Francisco Chinatown
This lecture will provide an overview of Kwon's book-in-progress, Making San Francisco Chinatown. Drawing on unexamined family and community archives, this project illuminates the rich history of artistic production by those who lived and worked in the neighborhood after the Great San Francisco Earthquake of 1906. What forms of accommodation, resistance, community, and refusal are engendered by living in a place shaped by American orientalism?  Marci Kwon is Associate Professor of Art History and Marta Sutton Weeks Faculty Scholar in the Humanities at Stanford University.  She also co-directs the university's Asian American Art initiative.  Her research and teaching address a range of topics in twentieth-century art, including the intersection of fine art and vernacular practice, theories of modernism, discourses of “folk” and “self-taught” art, and Asian American/diasporic art. She is the author of essays on artists including Joan Brown, BTS, Isamu Noguchi, and Martin Wong.
location: Kohlberg Hall Scheuer Room - Multi-Purpose Room

title: Meditation & Breathwork
description: Grounded in Creative Praxis’s healing-centered approach, this art-based session introduces practical meditation and breathwork techniques that restore calm, clarity, and focus. Drawing inspiration from the powerful mindfulness practices of Thích Nh?t H?nh, participants will explore how breath can serve as both an anchor and a liberatory tool. Using elements of poetry and visual arts, the session provides space to creatively reflect while learning culturally relevant strategies to regulate the nervous system, release stress, and cultivate presence in daily life. Through guided practice and reflection, we’ll discover how mindfulness and breathwork can nurture both personal wellness and collective care.
location: Hormel/Nguyen Intercultural Center at Sproul Hall Room 201 Dome Room

title: Palestinian Disposability and Contemporary Narrative
description:
Bashir Abu-Manneh, Cornell Distinguished Visiting Professor

(Lecturer at the School of Humanities, University of Kent (UK), where he served as Head of School from 2021 to 2024.)

 

Palestinian Disposability and Contemporary Narrative

WHEN: Wednesday, March 4, 2026, 4:30 pm

WHERE: Science Center 199

 

This talk argues that, as a result of Israel’s closure and separation policies institutionalized in the Oslo Peace Accords of 1993, Palestinians have become a disposable population and particularly vulnerable to increasingly extreme forms of domination, violence, and war since 2000. I delineate the reality of Palestinian disposability under colonial occupation and trace its literary representations in Palestinian narrative. I also ask what imaginative resources Palestinian writers mobilize to overcome disposability and to chart a future in which self-determination is possible. 

 

Bashir Abu-Manneh Bio: 

Dr. Bashir Abu-Manneh is Reader in Postcolonial Literature in the School of Humanities at the University of Kent (UK) and author of The Palestinian Novel: From 1948 to the Present (2016) and Fiction of the New Statesman, 1913-1939 (2011). He has also edited a collection of essays on Edward Said – as literary critic and postcolonial theorist – entitled After Said: Postcolonial Literary Studies in the Twenty-First Century (2019). His forthcoming book is entitled Disposable Palestinians: Israel’s Wars and Genocide in Gaza and will be published this Fall.

title: Inna Krasnoper: The Contexts of Translingualism: Poetry Reading and Conversation
description:
How do languages overlap, inform each other, and shake their settled places? How does the native language decenter itself? What is (en)count(er)ed in language mixing? What are the grounds to set and reflect poetic practice? Inna Krasnoper writes and publishes poetry in Russian, English, German and in combinations of those languages. She will speak about her poetic and artistic practices, moving between mediums, and ongoing translation/transition processes. The reading will include poems in the original and in translation, as well as performative experiments.

Inna Krasnoper is a poet, artist, and literary translator born in Ufa (Bashkortostan) and based in Berlin. She graduated from the Chto Delat Collective School of Engaged Art in Saint Petersburg and holds a BA in Dance, Context, Choreography from the Berlin University of the Arts. Her Russophone poetry collections include Nitki torchat (Loose Threads), published by the Voznesensky Center in 2021, and Dorogoy chelovek (Dear Person), published by NLO in 2024. Her poetry has also appeared in Vozdukh, [Translit], Zerkalo, F-pis'mo, and other periodicals, as well as in German, English, French, and Polish translation. Her multilingual poetry has appeared in her chapbook Over Sight (Eulalia Books), as well as in Annulet, Gulf Coast, StatORec, and elsewhere. Her first English-language poetry collection dis tanz was published by Veliz Books in 2025.

title: BlackStar Selects: Short Film Showcase and Filmmaker Q&A
description:
Film and Media Studies Presents

BlackStar Selects

Short Film Showcase and Filmmaker Q&A

Wednesday, March 4, 7pm

LPAC, Cinema

Swarthmore’s annual showcase of highlights from Philadelphia’s festival of films from the global majority includes four extraordinary short films. Talking Walls (Marcellus, 27 min.) weaves together tender and defiant stories of queer black elders. We Were the Scenery (Christopher Radcliff, 15 min) layers past and present in first-person accounts of Vietnamese refugees who appeared as extras in Apocalypse Now en route to full lives in the US. In Oceania (Valentin Noujaïm, 24 min.)  the death of an elderly neighbor who fought during the Algerian war prompts a reserved teenager to explore the man’s apartment and past romance with a member of the Black Panthers through found objects, letters, and his own imagination. The young couple in Food for the Soul (Chisom Chieke, 18 min.) navigates the challenges of their relationship, personal ambitions, and family pressures with homemade jollof and take-out soul food. 

Discussion with Philly-based filmmakers Marcellus and Chisom Chieke after the program moderated by Assistant Professor of Black Studies Jamal Batts.

Chisom Chieke is an Igbo-American multimedia artist and second-generation storyteller who writes, directs, and produces work that examines the past, present, and future of radical love, acceptance, and growth across Black diasporic communities. She is a 2025 BlackStar Filmmakers Lab Fellow.

Marcellus is an artist, filmmaker, media programmer and educator whose work focuses on archival and material notions of Blackness and queerness. Talking Walls won Best Audience Short Documentary and the Philadelphia Filmmaker Award at BlackStar 2025. 

Supported by the Dean’s Office for Inclusive Excellence  and the President’s Fund for Racial Justice. 

Cosponsored by the Black Studies Department and the Black Cultural Center. 

The program is free, accessible, and open to the public. Directions to Swarthmore College (link)

For more information, email: filmandmedia@swarthmore.edu
location: Lang Performing Arts Center Room (LPAC) 101 Cinema
organization: Department of Film and Media Studies

title: Inclusive Excellence Community Conversation
description:
Community Conversation: Processing our Community's Journey 

 


Please join us this academic year for the Community Conversation Series 2025-2026. This year, we will focus on Healing and Re-building Community.

 

Facilitated by Dean Tiffany Thompson and Vice President Brooke Vick, Community Conversations brings diverse community members together to discuss, listen, and learn from different perspectives. You will hear about other's lived experience and be able to share your own as well. Through open, honest, and respectful dialogue, we will begin building a stronger community. 

 


Fall Semester 2025

September 4th -  Processing our Community's Journey 

October 9th  - Repairing Harm: Pathways to Accountability and Reconciliation 

November 6th - Shared Values, Shared Future: Building a Culture of Trust and Belonging

Spring Semester 2026

February 5th- Re-imagining Our Campus Community: What Do We Want to Build?

March 5th - The College’s Role in Campus Healing

April 2nd - Allyship and Action: Moving from Awareness to Impact

title: CCC Requiem
description:
Join the Chester Children’s Chorus for a moving performance of Mozart’s Requiem on Sunday, March 8, 2026.

 

One of the most profound works in the choral repertoire, Mozart's Requiem will be performed alongside 30 community singers, professional soloists, and an orchestra, bringing together generations of voices and musicians.

 

This masterpiece, performed only every three to four years, holds special meaning for the Chorus and reflects the children's focus, discipline, and joy. 

 

Admission is free and open to the public.

 

Seating is limited and available on a first-come, first-served basis. 

 

Guests are invited to a reception following the concert in the lobby of the Lang Music Building.

 

The event will also be livestreamed on the CCC's Facebook and YouTube pages.

title: Exhibition: McCabe Library–from Dirt to Dust
description:
Dedicated in 1967, the Thomas B. and Jeanette L. McCabe Library has a history that began millions of years earlier, with the schist that forms its walls, and that will end untold years in the future, as it returns to the earth. In between, it is a building constantly in flux. Created as an act of permanence, to preserve the books and archives within its walls, McCabe has had to constantly adapt to the changing ways that people use libraries. Built by skilled hands that honed it from wood, concrete, and stone, many more hands make sure every day that it retains its function.

Curated by students in ARTH 067: Building Architecture from Dirt to Dust, this exhibition examines these many hands, including quarrymen and construction workers, custodians and archivists, architects and journalists, donors and woodworkers. Using the very archival documents housed in McCabe and some beyond, students follow ten themes (dirt, material, design, construction, capital, furnishing and decor, media, renovation, maintenance and care, and dust) that illuminate the myriad people and processes that have shaped and reshaped this familiar structure. In telling the many histories of McCabe Library from dirt to dust, the objects in this exhibition tell a larger story about all buildings—as the products of diverse forms of labor, shaped collaboratively, and with long lives that extend far into the past and future.

On display in the McCabe Cratsley Lounge (2nd Floor) through March 1.

title: Marriage Refuge: Shared Temporalities of Girlhood in Imperial Japanese Literature
description:
Marina Nascimento
Ph.D. Candidate, University of Pennsylvania

This talk will explore how Japanese-language literature across the Japanese Empire fostered a shared temporal imaginary for schoolgirls confronting the uncertain threshold between graduation and marriage. Focusing on Yoshiya Nobuko’s Yellow Rose (1923) and Yang Ch’ien-ho’s The Season When the Flowers Bloom (1942), I argue that girls’ fiction offered emotional vocabularies and narrative structures through which young women across the empire, whether in Tokyo or colonial Taiwan, grappled with similar anxieties around autonomy, desire, and (colonial) modernity.

title: G&A: Budgeting Workshop
description:
Please join Christy Brydges, the College’s Grant Accountant, for an exciting mini-training session on budget development for external grants. The budget is a crucial part of the proposal detailing the anticipated project costs. The budget showcases to the funding agency that we are equipped with the necessary resources to achieve the sponsored project’s goals and objectives. Who Should Attend?

Are you new to budget development or are you an experienced principal investigator (PI), perhaps looking to expand your budgeting knowledge and enhance your research portfolio? There is something for all!

title: Sigma Xi Talk with Navid Saleh
description:
Water, Wisdom, and Innovation: Blending Indigenous and Scientific Pathways for Sustainable Design

Technology alone cannot solve the water pollution crisis in local and Indigenous
communities. Its potential must be reconciled with the ethos of those it aims to serve and with the historical mistrust bred by extractive practices. The global environmental engineering and science community has long worked to decontaminate water, reduce salinity, and ensure a safe,
uninterrupted supply for millions. Yet, those outside centralized water systems — those who have experienced systemic injustice or been deprived of access to safe water — may still be left unserved by these efforts. At the same time, many such communities possess valuable holistic knowledge from which science can learn in addressing unresolved challenges. It would be audacious for any discipline scarcely half-a-millennium old to overlook knowledge forged over
seventy millennia. This talk proposes a framework converging Indigenous and classical engineering design pathways and aspires to serve as a catalyst for empathic design.

Biography: Navid Saleh is the Jewel McAlister Smith Endowed Professor
of Maseeh Department of Civil, Architectural and Environmental
Engineering at the University of Texas at Austin, USA. He received his
Ph.D. from Carnegie Mellon University and was trained as a postdoctoral
fellow at Yale University. Professor Saleh's research focuses on human-
centered empathic design of nano-enabled water treatment technologies.
Saleh research group works with Zapotec communities in Mexico, Navajo
and Alaska Natives in the US, and the Chakma community in Bangladesh.
Saleh’s research has produced 102 refereed publications and 7 book
chapters with more than 12,000 citations and an h-index of 45. Saleh’s
research has been recently featured in PBS NOVA’s documentary titled:
Building Stuff: Change it! He serves as an Editor for the Journal of Hazardous Materials and
Total Environment Engineering. Dr. Saleh also served as the President of the Sustainable Nanotechnology Organization. He sits on the Editorial Boards of Environmental Science & Technology and Environmental Science: Nano.

title: Cooper Series: Daedalus String Quartet Masterclass
description:
The Williams J. Cooper Series presents a masterclass with the Daedalus String Quartet on Wednesday, March 18th at 12noon in the Lang Concert Hall.  

Praised by The New Yorker as “a fresh and vital young participant in what is a golden age of American string quartets,” the Daedalus Quartet has established itself as a leader among the new generation of string ensembles. Since winning the top prize in the Banff International String Quartet Competition in 2001, the Daedalus Quartet has impressed critics and listeners alike with the security, technical finish, interpretive unity, and sheer gusto of its performances. The New York Times has praised the Daedalus Quartet’s “insightful and vibrant” Haydn, the “impressive intensity” of their Beethoven, their “luminous” Berg, and the “riveting focus” of their Dutilleux. The Washington Post in turn has acclaimed their performance of Mendelssohn for its “rockets of blistering virtuosity,” while the Houston Chronicle has described the “silvery beauty” of their Schubert and the “magic that hushed the audience” when they played Ravel, the Boston Globe the “finesse and fury” of their Shostakovich, the Toronto Globe and Mail the “thrilling revelation” of their Hindemith, and the Cincinnati Enquirer the “tremendous emotional power” of their Brahms.


Since its founding the Daedalus Quartet has performed in many of the world’s leading musical venues; in the United States and Canada these include Carnegie Hall, Lincoln Center (Great Performers series), the Library of Congress, the Corcoran Gallery in Washington, D.C., and Boston’s Gardner Museum, as well as on major series in Montreal, Toronto, Calgary, Winnipeg, and Vancouver. Abroad the ensemble has been heard in such famed locations as the Musikverein in Vienna, the Mozarteum in Salzburg, the Concertgebouw in Amsterdam, the Cité de la Musique in Paris, and in leading venues in Japan.


The Daedalus Quartet has won plaudits for its adventurous exploration of and commitment to contemporary music. Among the works the ensemble has premiered are Huck Hodge's The Topography of Desire, commissioned by the Fromm Foundation; Joan Tower’s White Water, commissioned by Chamber Music Monterey Bay; David Horne’s Flight from the Labyrinth, commissioned for the Quartet by the Caramoor Festival; Lawrence Dillon’s String Quartet No. 4, commissioned by the Thomas S. Kenan Institute for the Arts; and Fred Lerdahl’s Third String Quartet, commissioned by Chamber Music America, as well as Lerdahl's Chaconne, commissioned by New Music USA.


The Quartet has also collaborated with some of the world’s finest instrumentalists: these include pianists Marc-André Hamelin, Simone Dinnerstein, Awadagin Pratt, Joyce Yang, and Benjamin Hochman; clarinetists Paquito D’Rivera, Ricardo Morales, Romie deGuise-Langlois, and Alexander Fiterstein; jazz bassist John Patitucci; and violists Roger Tapping, Nokuthula Ngwenyama, and Donald Weilerstein.


To date the Quartet has forged associations with some of America’s leading classical music and educational institutions: Carnegie Hall, through its European Concert Hall Organization (ECHO) Rising Stars program; and Lincoln Center, which appointed the Daedalus Quartet as the Chamber Music Society Two (now the Bowers Program) quartet for 2005-07. The Daedalus Quartet has served as Quartet-in-Residence at the University of Pennsylvania since 2006. In 2007, the Quartet was awarded Lincoln Center’s Martin E. Segal Award. The Quartet won Chamber Music America’s Guarneri String Quartet Award, which funded a three-year residency in Suffolk County, Long Island from 2007-2010.


The quartet’s debut recording, music of Stravinsky, Sibelius, and Ravel, was released by Bridge Records in 2006, and was praised by Strad Magazine for the quartet's "exemplary intonation and balance." Other recordings the quartet has released include music of Haydn, Fred Lerdahl, Arlene Sierra, George Perle, and Ursula Mamlock (for Bridge Records), music of Joan Tower (for Naxos), and Debra Kaye (for Navona Records). In the past season the Quartet recorded a string quartet and oboe quintet (with oboist James Austin Smith) by Anna Weesner, Huck Hodge’s Topography of Desire, and Nathan Courtright’s On remembering, ceasing to be (with the Arcana New Music Ensemble).


Highlights of the 23/24 season include two premieres and the quartet’s first Bartok cycle. Daedalus will premiere Stewart Goodyear’s piano quintet, with the composer at the piano, at the Philadelphia Chamber Music Society, and will also perform as part of Lyric Fest’s Christmas Cantata, in a new work by composer Benjamin Wenzelberg on poetry of Jeanne Minahan for Philadelphia’s Lyric Fest. At the University of Pennsylvania, the Quartet will embark on a multi-disciplinary investigation of the music of Bartok, featuring each of the six quartets contextualized in various ways.


The members of the Daedalus Quartet hold degrees from the Juilliard School, Curtis Institute, Cleveland Institute, and Harvard University.

title: Genevieve Ching-wen Lee '96 Memorial Lecture
description:
Swarthmore College

Asian American Studies presents

2026 Genevieve Ching-wen Lee '96 Memorial Lecture
"How Asian American Studies Can Help You Fight Fascism: Joy as Resistance"

Wednesday, March 18
4:30 p.m. lecture with reception to follow

Sproul 201, Intercultural Center

 

Dr. Jennifer Ho
Professor, Ethnic Studies
Director, Center for Humanities & the Arts
University of Colorado Boulder

Past President, Association for Asian American Studies (2020-2022)

----

The daughter of a refugee father from China and an immigrant mother from Jamaica, whose parents themselves were immigrants from Hong Kong, Jennifer Ho is the director of the Center for Humanities & the Arts and Professor of Ethnic Studies at the University of Colorado Boulder, where she specializes in Asian American literary and cultural studies and Critical Race Theory. She is past president of the Association for Asian American Studies (2020-2022), the editor of four essay collections, the author of three scholarly monographs and a number of essays and articles, both research oriented and public facing. In addition to her academic work, Ho is active in community engagement around issues of race and intersectionality.

Dr. Ho will discuss how in the 1960s, activists of largely Japanese, Chinese, Filipino/a/x, and Korean descent came together out of radical organizing to create an umbrella term “Asian American” in their fight for ethnic studies, anti-war protest, and solidarity with Black civil rights. From that movement grew a field of study with roots in social justice activism and political organizing. Over 60 years later the field of Asian American Studies has many lessons for anyone interested in fighting against various forms of fascism, such as the role of joy (especially through art, food, and music) in resisting authoritarianism. Dr. Jennifer Ho will share observations rooted in her professional and personal experiences with Asian American studies as a tool to fight fascism and to promote joy as resistance in the authoritarian times we find ourselves in.

title: Exhibition: McCabe Library–from Dirt to Dust
description:
Dedicated in 1967, the Thomas B. and Jeanette L. McCabe Library has a history that began millions of years earlier, with the schist that forms its walls, and that will end untold years in the future, as it returns to the earth. In between, it is a building constantly in flux. Created as an act of permanence, to preserve the books and archives within its walls, McCabe has had to constantly adapt to the changing ways that people use libraries. Built by skilled hands that honed it from wood, concrete, and stone, many more hands make sure every day that it retains its function.

Curated by students in ARTH 067: Building Architecture from Dirt to Dust, this exhibition examines these many hands, including quarrymen and construction workers, custodians and archivists, architects and journalists, donors and woodworkers. Using the very archival documents housed in McCabe and some beyond, students follow ten themes (dirt, material, design, construction, capital, furnishing and decor, media, renovation, maintenance and care, and dust) that illuminate the myriad people and processes that have shaped and reshaped this familiar structure. In telling the many histories of McCabe Library from dirt to dust, the objects in this exhibition tell a larger story about all buildings—as the products of diverse forms of labor, shaped collaboratively, and with long lives that extend far into the past and future.

On display in the McCabe Cratsley Lounge (2nd Floor) through March 1.

Please join us this academic year for the Lunch and Learn Series 2025-2026. Focusing on Healing and Re-building Community, we have invited Creative Praxis to Swarthmore to guide us through this year's series. 

 

Creative Praxis is an arts-based, healing-centered, and joy-filled training organization. Their programs are designed to inspire and connect people, fostering personal and community transformation. With over 60 years of combined experience, their facilitators are experts in interactive, transformational facilitation. The founder, Nia Eubanks-Dixon, is certified in restorative practices, trauma healing, and de-escalation techniques.

 

September 11th - Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care  - An interactive session that challenges the fear of conflict for growth and connection. Learn culturally relevant strategies to navigate conflict with empathy, compassion, and clarity. When conflict is approached with intention, honesty, and care, we strengthen relationships and build healthier communities.

title: Helen North Memorial Lecture: To Cut Up Nightingales: the Disorientation of Helen Magill, American Classicist
description: Constanze Guthenke, Associate Professor of Greek Literature at Oxford University and E.P. Warren Praelector at Corpus Christi College, will present the twenty-third Helen North Memorial Lecture in Classics.  Helen Magill (1853-1944), a member of Swarthmore's first graduating class in 1873, was the first woman to receive a PhD at a U.S. institution, the co-educational Boston University in 1877. Her trajectory opens up critical questions about the imagination of what a professional classicist could be; about the triangulated relationship between America, Europe, and the ancient world; and about the highly ambivalent attitude towards specialist research.  All are welcome.

description:
Performances

Friday, March 20, 8 p.m. 

LPAC, Pearson-Hall Theatre 

 

Saturday, March 21, 2 p.m. and 8 p.m. 

LPAC, Pearson-Hall Theatre 

 

Inspired by Jay Kirk’s Avoid the Day, the Daedalus Quartet joins forces with Pig Iron Theatre Company and visual artist Sebastienne Mundheim to present Bartok’s Monster, a concert and sly lecture-demonstration about Béla Bartók’s life and work. This kaleidoscopic performance follows Kirk's research into the Hungarian composer's methods of creation, travels to Transylvania, and ultimately flirtation with madness. Paper sculptures by Mundheim and a movement ensemble including current Swarthmore students frame a not-so-academic lecture in this one-hour multidisciplinary performance.

title: The Model Migrant and the Colonial Mindset: Narratives of German Settlement in the Midwest and Africa
description:
The nineteenth century was characterized by German migration to different parts of the world, including the United States and some parts of Africa. At the same time, Germany, drawing from American Westward expansion, became a colonial power and subsequently, the third most powerful colonizing nation after Britain and France. This presentation examines the role of popular media in creating and sustaining an image of Germans both as model migrants and as unique colonizers. The presentation critiques these two narratives through the coverage of historical events in the United States and in Africa at the Turn of the Century. It also pays attention to the connection between German colonial exploits in Africa and the Third Reich.

Bio:

Dr. Peter Ogunniran is an Assistant Professor in the Department of German and Russian at Williams College. He holds a PhD in German and Comparative Literature, and a graduate certificate in Higher Education Administration from Washington University in Saint Louis. He completed his master’s in Modern Languages at the University of Mississippi and received his bachelor’s degree in European Studies (German) from the University of Ibadan, Ibadan Nigeria.

At Williams College, he teaches German language classes at different levels as well as seminars including (In)Justice and Morals in German Literature, The Other Germans: Exploring Diversity in Contemporary German Literature, Literature as Resistance, Heimat: Identity, Belonging and Home in German Literature, as well as Europe and the Black Diaspora.

His research areas include German colonial literature and history, nationalism, public media and visual culture, Afro-German studies and family narratives. His current research investigates the mediation of nationalism and the colonial Other in German colonies in the late 19th and early 20th centuries; the roles of public media such as family magazine, literature for children and adults; and the role of public spaces in mediating nationalism and national identity.

title: Sixteen Years - Wrongful Convictions and the Fight for Justice: Film Screening and Panel Discussion
description: In January of 1991, Jeffrey Deskovic, then 18 years old, was convicted of the murder and rape of a classmate. His conviction, based on forced confessions and false claims despite exculpatory DNA evidence, led him to spend sixteen years of his life in prison. Jeffrey Deskovic is now a powerful attorney and a leader in the field of innocence work, fighting for justice in the cases of others who have experienced similar wrongdoings at the hands of a system meant to provide equal protection and justice to all. Join Youth for Innocence Swarthmore for a screening of Sixteen Years, a documentary telling Jeffrey Deskovic’s story of wrongful conviction and his continued fight for justice. After the film, we will be hosting a panel discussion with Jeffrey Deskovic and Jia Rizvi, the filmmaker of Sixteen Years and a powerful voice on wrongful convictions. Her debut documentary, Conviction, which explores the shortcomings of the criminal legal system, screened at 17 festivals worldwide and won six awards, including Best Picture and Best Cinematography. Ms. Rizvi also hosts the true crime podcast Speaking of Crime, has authored more than 300 articles for Forbes and has hosted episodes of the Emmy Award-winning show The List.

title: CS is Dead. Long Live CS.
description:
"Over the last 10-15 years, almost everybody who sits on a stage like this would tell you that it is vital that your children learn computer science; everybody should learn how to program. And it's almost exactly the opposite.“ Jensen Huang, CEO NVIDIA, 2024

“Big Tech Told Kids to Code. The Jobs Didn’t Follow.” New York Times The Daily. September 2025.

“We’re considering scaling back our coding curriculum to make room for AI literacy instead. After all, these students will likely be prompting AI to write code rather than writing it themselves.” Education Week, September 2025.

We’ve heard this story before. The argument is always the same: the tools have gotten so good that understanding what's underneath no longer matters. And the argument is always wrong.

In this talk, I'll make the case that computer science isn't just still relevant in the age of AI; it's foundational knowledge that every student will need. I'll discuss how the Computer Science Teachers Association developed and continues to evolve the CSTA K-12 standards, how we are navigating the pressure to treat "AI literacy" as a standalone discipline, and why stripping CS out of that conversation puts students at risk of becoming passive consumers rather than informed creators.

I'll also share my own path from Swarthmore CS graduate to leading a national education nonprofit as one example of how a CS background opens doors far beyond the tech industry.

Finally, participants will work through an activity designed to surface a deceptively hard question: What are the core concepts and competencies that a student entering kindergarten today will need to thrive when they enter the workforce in 2040? The answers should shape how we teach computer science right now.

This talk is for anyone who cares about the future of CS education. Whether you’re a CS major heading to industry, an educator heading into your first classroom, or a future policy leader who wants to improve equitable education for all this talk is for you.


Speaker Bio
Jake Baskin '10 is Executive Director of the Computer Science Teachers Association (CSTA), the largest membership organization for CS educators in the world. Under his leadership, CSTA has grown from insolvency to a thriving $10M+ organization with 10,000+ members and chapters across three continents. Jake has represented CS educators at the White House, the National Governors Association, and international conferences. Previously, he helped build Code.org into a national movement for K-12 computer science education. Jake got his start teaching CS and coaching basketball at Lindblom Math and Science Academy, a Chicago Public School. His path began with a computer science degree (and a lot of poetry classes) from Swarthmore. He's excited to be back on campus.

title: Saffran Lecture - Laura Barasa
description:
Department of Economics - Saffran Lecture
Laura Barasa, Ph.D.
Department of Economics and Development Studies
University of Nairobi

Seeing Development from the Other Side

Growing up in Kenya, a lower-middle-income country in East Africa, Dr. Barasa saw firsthand how opportunity is shaped not only by income, but also by access to education and good governance. In this talk, Dr. Barasa draws on her work in development economics to examine the Global North–Global South divide in both economic outcomes and the production of scientific knowledge. Using examples from research on post-election violence, gambling, and governance, she highlights how scholarship from the Global South challenges dominant narratives and expands how we think about development. This lecture invites us to question whose data and experiences inform economics, and how broadening scholarship can open new pathways for opportunity across regions.

McCabe Library has a history that began millions of years earlier, with the schist that forms its walls, and that will end untold years in the future, as it returns to the earth. In between, it is a building constantly in flux. Created as an act of permanence, to preserve the books and archives within its walls, McCabe has had to constantly adapt to the changing ways that people use libraries. Built by skilled hands that honed it from wood, concrete, and stone, many more hands make sure every day that it retains its function.

Curated by students in ARTH 067: Building Architecture from Dirt to Dust, this exhibition examines these many hands, including quarrymen and construction workers, custodians and archivists, architects and journalists, donors and woodworkers. Using the very archival documents housed in McCabe and some beyond, students follow ten themes (dirt, material, design, construction, capital, furnishing and decor, media, renovation, maintenance and care, and dust) that illuminate the myriad people and processes that have shaped and reshaped this familiar structure. In telling the many histories of McCabe Library from dirt to dust, the objects in this exhibition tell a larger story about all buildings—as the products of diverse forms of labor, shaped collaboratively, and with long lives that extend far into the past and future.

On display in the McCabe Cratsley Lounge (2nd Floor) through March 1.

title: American Martyr: A New Cantata
description:
Concert

Friday, March 27, 8 p.m.

Lang Music Building, Lang Concert Hall

 

World premiere vocal performance, an original work for soloists, chorus, piano, and organ by composer C. Leonard Raybon. Inspired by Murder in a Mill Town by Professor Bruce Dorsey, American Martyr tells the tale of Sarah Maria Cornell – tossed about by her friends, family, and God – searching desperately for love and faith in a life cut short. In this new work, she is made whole, beatified. Featuring vocal performances by students and alums. Presented by Swarthmore’s Music and History Departments.

description:
Come sample some of Philly's best doughnuts (Beiler's) and learn about the Fall 2026 Tri-Co Philly Program. This semester-long program offers students courses and a host of activities in Philadelphia. Students take two urban-focused, experiential courses taught in Philadelphia, and also participate in monthly Philadelphia-based activities. Students who will be sophomores, juniors, and seniors in Fall 2026 are eligible to apply.

FALL 2026 COURSES | Program students choose 2 of 3 courses, others can take a single course.


  • Ecologies of Cooperation: Modeling Participation and Ecological Practice Through Philadelphia-Based Arts, Culture, and Environmental Co-Operatives | ENVS/VIST H317A
  • Biostatistics: Data, Health and Ethics in Philadelphia | MATH B195 
  • Art and Culture of Indigenous Philadelphia: From Shackamaxon to the Present | ARTH061/ENVS056

APPLYING TO TRI-CO PHILLY:


  1. Interested students must attend a Tri-Co Philly Program Student Information Session. There is one scheduled at each campus this spring.

Haverford: Monday, March 23, 2026 | 4:30-5:30 p.m. | Union Building 111
Bryn Mawr: Monday, March 30, 2026 | 4:30-5:30 p.m. | Dalton Hall 025
Swarthmore: Wednesday, April 1, 2026 | 4:30-5:30 p.m. | SCI 158
Online: Thursday, April 2, 2026 | 7-8 p.m. | Register


  1. After attending one Tri-Co Philly Program Student Information Session, students can apply for the Tri-Co Philly Program. Students unable to attend an information session must meet with the Tri-Co Philly Program director, Calista Cleary (ccleary1@swarthmore.edu or 610-795-1576), prior to applying.
  2. The Tri-Co Philly application is due Sunday, April 5, by noon. Note: this is before all Tri-Co registration periods.

Community Conversation: Processing our Community's Journey 

 


Please join us this academic year for the Community Conversation Series 2025-2026. This year, we will focus on Healing and Re-building Community.

 

Facilitated by Dean Tiffany Thompson and Vice President Brooke Vick, Community Conversations brings diverse community members together to discuss, listen, and learn from different perspectives. You will hear about other's lived experience and be able to share your own as well. Through open, honest, and respectful dialogue, we will begin building a stronger community. 

 


Fall Semester 2025

September 4th -  Processing our Community's Journey 

October 9th  - Repairing Harm: Pathways to Accountability and Reconciliation 

November 6th - Shared Values, Shared Future: Building a Culture of Trust and Belonging

Spring Semester 2026

February 5th- Re-imagining Our Campus Community: What Do We Want to Build?

March 5th - The College’s Role in Campus Healing

April 2nd - Allyship and Action: Moving from Awareness to Impact

title: Online Tri-Co Philly Student Info Session
description:
Looking for a different kind of learning? Curious how your academic work connects to people's lived experience? Come discover the Fall 2026 Tri-Co Philly Program, which connects students to the city. Students take two urban-focused, experiential courses taught in Philadelphia, and also participate in monthly Philadelphia-based activities. Students who will be sophomores, juniors, and seniors in Fall 2026 are eligible to apply.

FALL 2026 COURSES | Program students choose 2 of 3 courses, others can take a single course (no info session or application needed).


  • Ecologies of Cooperation: Modeling Participation and Ecological Practice Through Philadelphia-Based Arts, Culture, and Environmental Co-Operatives | ENVS/VIST H317A | Also counts toward: Growth & Structure of Cities
  • Biostatistics: Data, Health and Ethics in Philadelphia | MATH B195 | Also counts toward: Data Science, Health Studies (M-track), Growth & Structure of Cities
  • Art and Culture of Indigenous Philadelphia: From Shackamaxon to the Present | ARTH061/ENVS056

Tri-Co Philly Program Student Info Session Details | Interested students should attend an info session.
Swarthmore: Wednesday, April 1, 2026 | 4:30-5:30 p.m. | SCI 158
Online: Thursday, April 2, 2026 | 7-8 p.m. | Register

After attending an info session, students can apply for the Tri-Co Philly Program. The application deadline is Sunday, April 5, by noon. Students unable to attend an information session should meet with the Tri-Co Philly Program director, Calista Cleary, prior to applying.

title: SSQL Workshop: Data Visualization in R
description: This workshop will focus on how to visually explore and communicate your data in R. You will learn key techniques for creating effective charts and graphs, choosing appropriate visualizations for different types of data, and interpreting the patterns you see. We will cover essential packages like ggplot2, customizing plots, combining multiple plots, and best practices for making your visuals clear and informative. By the end of the session, you'll be able to generate publication-quality graphics and use data visualization as a powerful tool for analysis and storytelling.

title: Arabic Language Table
description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. It fosters spontaneous speech, reinforces vocabulary in context, and encourages cultural exploration through discussions on music, films, and current events. By creating a sense of community, it connects students across proficiency levels, motivating them to participate and apply their skills in real-world interactions. This informal setting helps bridge classroom learning with practical use, preparing students for study abroad, internships, and conversations with native speakers. Ultimately, it enhances fluency, cultural awareness, and a deeper appreciation for the Arabic language.

title: GEO Cape Town Consortium Information Session
description:
The Consortium Program, established in 2003, offers students of Macalester, Pomona, and Swarthmore Colleges (and their affiliated institutions) the opportunity to study the environment and complex, multi-cultural society of South Africa against a background of globalization and rapid technological change. The program takes place at the University of Cape Town, a world-class institution where professors, in consultation with those from the consortium colleges, have developed a curriculum that meets the high academic standards of our schools while at the same time facilitating the challenge of living and studying in a foreign country. The program is hosted by the Department of Environmental and Geographical Science (EGS) at the University of Cape Town (UCT), which has an internationally respected student-faculty team with strong areas of interest in issues such as environmental and climate change, Global Information Systems, urban and agricultural development, and conservation of biodiversity. Cape Town itself is a breathtakingly beautiful city nestled against Table Mountain at the southwestern tip of Africa.

 

This is a spring only program.

 

Join us for Q&A with Prof. Carr Everbach, Andie D'Agostino, Assistant Director in GEO, and returned study abroad students.

title: Judd Kessler Lecture
description:
Department of Economics

Judd B. Kessler, Ph.D.
Howard Marks Professor
Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania 

Lucky by Design
Book Talk

Wharton economist Judd Kessler discusses his new book, “Lucky by Design: The Hidden Economics You Need to Get More of What You Want.”

Professor Kessler pulls back the curtain on the hidden markets that determine who gets what in everyday life. Unlike familiar markets that you might learn about in your economics class — where what we get depends on how much we’re willing to pay — hidden markets do not rely on prices: you can’t buy your way into a better position. Instead, what you receive hinges on the rules by which the market operates, and the choices you make in them.

What are the tricks for getting reservations at the hottest restaurants, live-event tickets, job offers, and spots in elite preschools and selective colleges? What about finding a soulmate on a dating app or receiving a life-saving organ transplant? How can policymakers design these markets better?

Professor Kessler has spent a career studying and designing these very markets. Now, he reveals insights about how they work, how to maneuver in them, and how to tip the scales in your favor.

Complimentary copies of Professor Kessler's book will be available on a first-come, first-served basis.

title: Inclusive Excellence Lunch & Learns
description:
Lunch and Learn: Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care

 


Please join us this academic year for the Lunch and Learn Series 2025-2026. Focusing on Healing and Re-building Community, we have invited Creative Praxis to Swarthmore to guide us through this year's series. 

 

Creative Praxis is an arts-based, healing-centered, and joy-filled training organization. Their programs are designed to inspire and connect people, fostering personal and community transformation. With over 60 years of combined experience, their facilitators are experts in interactive, transformational facilitation. The founder, Nia Eubanks-Dixon, is certified in restorative practices, trauma healing, and de-escalation techniques.

 

September 11th - Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care  - An interactive session that challenges the fear of conflict for growth and connection. Learn culturally relevant strategies to navigate conflict with empathy, compassion, and clarity. When conflict is approached with intention, honesty, and care, we strengthen relationships and build healthier communities.

title: On the Air in Changing Times – A German Correspondent in the U.S
description:
Please join us for a talk by Christina Eck, U.S. Radio Correspondent for the German Press Agency DPA in Washington, DC. Christina has also worked as a freelance correspondent and producer for German Public Radio ARD in Washington, DC, as a Reporter, Anchor, and Editor for Antenne Bayern Radio in Munich Germany, and a senior Editor, Anchor, and International Broadcaster for the German Service of Voice of America in Washington, DC.
Ms. Eck has been busy covering US-news for German radio for more than 30 years and she will take you on a little time travel, focusing on the continuity and change of news and journalism over the decades.
This event is sponsored by the German Studies section of the Department of Modern Languages and Literatures and the Department of Film and Media Studies.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Senior Case 2026
description:
The Senior Showcase provides a space where the Swarthmore community comes together to learn from senior projects across all three divisions. Participating students each have three minutes and one slide to present their work. Why only three minutes when our students could spend hours sharing the details of their projects? Most people will not take more than three minutes to listen and understand a student's work; therefore, students need to learn both how to distill their work into a three-minute sound bite and how to explain their work to a more general audience. Three minutes also allows for more participation from interested seniors.

The Senior Showcase from 3:30pm-4:30

Swat Poster Session: 4:30-5:30 (Sci Commons)

Refreshments will be served after the showcase.

As the lights dim and the music pulses through your veins, prepare to be swept away on a whirlwind of glamour, humor, and unapologetic fabulousness. Whether you're a seasoned drag aficionado or a first-time reveler, our show promises an unforgettable evening of laughter, love, and pure, unadulterated fun. title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.
